moveAnnotations.annotation: We begin with the Zukertort Opening, a flexible first move that controls the crucial central square e5 while keeping our options open—we're not committing to a particular pawn structure yet. This move immediately contests the center and prepares to support our pieces harmoniously. Historically favored by world champions like Kasparov, Nf3 is the hallmark of patient, positional players who build gradually rather than force early tactics.

Black mirrors our approach with the natural developing move Nf6, controlling e4 and d5 while preparing kingside castling. This is a sound, flexible response that doesn't reveal Black's central intentions—they keep the option of playing ...e6 (closed) or ...e5 (open) depending on what we do next.

Now we commit to the Nimzo-Larsen Variation, a hypermodern approach where we'll control the center from afar with our fianchettoed bishop on b2 rather than occupying it with pawns. This move prepares to fianchetto on the long diagonal, indirectly pressuring e5 and d4, while keeping our center flexible—we'll let Black decide how to occupy the middle while we build a solid positional setup.
